title: Handling XML Fragments
text:
Handling XML Fragments XML fragments are pieces of XML-encoded data in the content of elements mapped to Uniface fields. xmlsave and xmlload cannot handle them properly unless the DTD for fields containing XML mark-up is modifed to allow a partial copy of the data. This allows the data to be passed directly between the component and the XML stream, without parsing or additional translation or substitution by Uniface. Extracting fragments of XML from an XML stream is sometimes necessary. For example, an XML stream can be an envelope containing a message, and you want to access the message. At other times, you may want to load XML fragments into an XML stream. For example, a database table can store XHTML fragments. However, both xmlsave and xmlload modify data that contains data fragments: xmlsave substitutes XML entities for reserved characters in string data, to ensure that well-formed XML is generated. For example, the angle brackets ( < and > ) for each element are replaced with < and > respectively. All XML structure in string fields is lost. xmlload loads the first element encountered in an XML fragment into the target component field. All content before or after this element is discarded and all content inside this element is preserved, including comments, processing instructions, and attributes. For example, if you save a fragment such as: abcd efgh Copy then Uniface loads only efgh into the component field (ignoring the text abcd ). Using the Partial Copy To retain markup when including an XML fragment in a stream, Uniface provides a partial copy mechanism. To make use of it: Create a customized variant of your DTD. Map the field containing the XML fragment to an element containing a dummy child element. You can give this element any name that does not occur elsewhere in the DTD. When saving to an XML stream using the partial copy mechanism, the field content is copied unchanged (and unparsed) to the XML stream. If the field content is invalid or not well-formed, the resulting XML stream is also invalid or not well-formed. For example, if the field DESCR.ART contains the following well-formed XML fragment:

This description uses XHTML markup.

Copy You could use the following DTD declarations to ensure that the XML markup is preserved during xmlsave : ... ... Copy The element is added to the DTD to indicate that the DESCR.ART field contains markup. Figure 1. Partial Copy in Action Note: When you use the partial copy mechanism to insert XML fragments directly into an XML stream, Uniface does not ensure that the resulting XML stream is valid or well-formed. To avoid data loss during inter-component communication, ensure that all XML fragments saved into an XML stream are enclosed by an element, because Uniface loads only the first element encountered in an XML fragment during xmlload . ( xmlload loads well-formed XML fragments). Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
